questions.question: What does non-destructive hydration (the default since v16) do with the server-rendered DOM?
options: Reuses the server DOM, attaching listeners and wiring state in place
Throws the server markup away and re-renders the whole app from scratch
Renders a second copy into a hidden node and swaps it in
Keeps the server DOM but disables all event handling until a reload
questions.explanation: Non-destructive hydration reuses the server-rendered DOM and attaches listeners in place. The destructive re-render that discarded the markup was the pre-v16 behavior.

questions.question: Which provider call enables non-destructive client hydration in `app.config.ts`?
options: `provideClientHydration()`
`provideServerRendering()`
`provideHydration()`
`provideHttpClient(withHydration())`
questions.explanation: `provideClientHydration()` turns on non-destructive hydration (DOM reconciliation, transfer-state caching, and incremental hydration). `provideServerRendering()` is the server-side provider, not the hydration one.

questions.question: Which `RenderMode` renders a route to static HTML files at build time (SSG)?
options: `RenderMode.Prerender`
`RenderMode.Server`
`RenderMode.Client`
`RenderMode.Static`
questions.explanation: `RenderMode.Prerender` is SSG: rendered once at build time to static HTML. `RenderMode.Server` renders fresh HTML per request, and `RenderMode.Client` renders in the browser.

questions.question: What problem does `withEventReplay()` solve?
options: User events (e.g. clicks) that fire before hydration finishes would otherwise be lost
Server-fetched data would be requested a second time on the client
`@defer` blocks would all hydrate at once instead of lazily
Invalid HTML would force a destructive re-render of the page
questions.explanation: `withEventReplay()` captures early user events that fire before listeners are attached and replays them once hydration completes. Avoiding a duplicate fetch is transfer state's job, not event replay's.

questions.question: On the state escalation ladder, what is the recommended starting point before promoting state anywhere else?
options: A `signal` owned locally by the component
An `@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })` signal service
NgRx SignalStore
A classic NgRx Store with actions and reducers
questions.explanation: The ladder starts with local component state (a signal owned by the component), promoting to a shared root service only when more than one component needs the same source of truth.

questions.question: In Angular v22 with a plain `provideClientHydration()`, what is the state of incremental hydration for `@defer` blocks?
options: On by default; the old `withIncrementalHydration()` opt-in is no longer required
Off until you add `withIncrementalHydration()`
Off until you add `withEventReplay()` separately
Unavailable — incremental hydration was removed in v22
questions.explanation: Since v22 incremental hydration is on by default with `provideClientHydration()`; the earlier `withIncrementalHydration()` opt-in is no longer needed (and you disable it with `withNoIncrementalHydration()`).

questions.question: Transfer state serializes data the server fetched via `HttpClient` into the HTML so the client does not re-issue the same request during hydration.
questions.explanation: That is exactly what transfer state does; it can be disabled per need with `withNoHttpTransferCache()`.

questions.question: Which of the following can cause a hydration mismatch? (Select all that apply.)
options: Direct DOM manipulation such as setting `innerHTML` or calling `appendChild`
Invalid HTML the browser auto-corrects, like a `<table>` without `<tbody>`
Third-party scripts mutating the DOM before hydration runs
Reading a `computed` signal inside the template
Using `OnPush` change detection on a component
questions.explanation: Direct DOM manipulation, invalid auto-corrected HTML, and third-party DOM mutation before hydration all cause mismatches (along with whitespace/comment-node differences). Reading a computed signal and using OnPush are normal and do not cause mismatches.

questions.question: When Angular detects a hydration mismatch in a subtree, what happens?
options: It falls back to a full client re-render for that subtree and logs a warning
It throws a fatal error and stops bootstrapping the application
It silently keeps the server DOM and never attaches listeners there
It re-runs the server render for that route on the next request
questions.explanation: A mismatch makes Angular discard and re-render that subtree on the client and log a warning — it degrades gracefully rather than crashing or silently dropping interactivity.

questions.question: In the v20+ API, which call wires per-route render modes for hybrid rendering on the server?
options: `provideServerRendering(withRoutes(serverRoutes))` from `@angular/ssr`
`provideServerRouting(serverRoutes)` from `@angular/platform-server`
`provideClientHydration(withRoutes(serverRoutes))`
`provideRouter(serverRoutes)` from `@angular/router`
questions.explanation: The v20+ API is `provideServerRendering(withRoutes(serverRoutes))`, with `provideServerRendering` now exported from `@angular/ssr`. The older `provideServerRouting` was replaced/removed in v20.

questions.question: In the `CartStore` below, why is `items` kept private while `lines`, `count`, and `total` are exposed?
options: So consumers can read state and derived values but only the service's methods may mutate the source
Because `computed` signals cannot be created from a public writable signal
Because `providedIn: 'root'` services forbid public writable fields
So that change detection skips the component reading `count`
questions.explanation: Keeping the writable signal private and exposing read-only views (`asReadonly()`/`computed`) enforces one source of truth: only the service's `add`/`clear` methods may mutate state. The other claims are not real Angular constraints.

questions.question: Why does `add()` use `this.items.update(list => [...list, item])` instead of `this.items().push(item)`?
options: Replacing the array gives a new reference so the signal notifies its dependents
`push` is not available on signal-wrapped arrays at runtime
Spreading is required to keep the array sorted
`update` runs the mutation off the main thread
questions.explanation: Signals detect change by reference; replacing the array with a new one triggers notification. Mutating in place with `push` would not change the reference, so dependents (computed/effects) would not react.

questions.question: What is the consequence of supplying an unstable `track` key on a `@for` loop that reorders its items?
options: Angular re-creates the DOM and component state for the rows instead of reusing the existing nodes
Angular throws a compile-time error and the template fails to build
The list renders correctly but `OnPush` is silently disabled for that component
Angular keeps the old DOM and refuses to apply the new ordering
questions.explanation: Without a stable identity, Angular re-creates DOM and state on reorder; a stable key like `track x.id` lets it reuse nodes. `track` is syntactically required for `@for`, so the real performance harm comes from giving it an unstable key rather than omitting it.

questions.question: Which statements about `NgOptimizedImage` (`ngSrc`) are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: It requires `width`/`height` (or `fill`) to prevent layout shift (CLS)
It lazy-loads non-priority images by default
You mark the LCP image with `priority` so it is not lazy-loaded
It defers image rendering until the user hovers the element
It disables `srcset` generation to keep the HTML small
questions.explanation: `NgOptimizedImage` requires dimensions (or `fill`) to avoid CLS, lazy-loads non-priority images, auto-generates `srcset`, and lets you flag the LCP image `priority`. It does not hover-defer images, and it generates `srcset` rather than disabling it.

questions.question: With `ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush` and signal reads in the template, when is the component re-checked?
options: When an input reference changes, an event fires in it, or a signal it reads changes
On every change-detection cycle, exactly as with default strategy
Only when you call `markForCheck()` manually after each mutation
Only when a parent component is re-checked, regardless of signals
questions.explanation: OnPush re-checks on an input reference change, an event in the component, or a change to a signal it reads. Signals make OnPush correct without manual `markForCheck()`, which is the trap in the third option.

questions.question: A binding like `{{ formatTotal() }}` that calls a component method runs on every change-detection cycle, whereas a pure pipe with the same logic recomputes only when its input changes.
questions.explanation: Template method calls re-run every CD cycle; pure pipes are memoized and recompute only when their input changes — which is why formatting/filtering should move into pure pipes (or `computed`).

questions.question: Why should you avoid allocating new arrays or objects inline in template bindings (e.g. `[data]="[a, b]"`)?
options: A fresh reference every cycle defeats `OnPush` input reference checks, forcing needless re-checks downstream
Inline literals are a syntax error inside Angular templates
It prevents the AOT compiler from tree-shaking the component
It causes a hydration mismatch on the server-rendered HTML
questions.explanation: An inline literal creates a new reference on every CD cycle, so a child relying on `OnPush` reference equality always sees a 'changed' input and re-checks unnecessarily. Precompute with `computed` or a pure pipe instead.

questions.question: Which statement accurately describes zoneless change detection?
options: It drops Zone.js so CD is driven by signals/events directly; stable since v20.2 and the default in v21+
It keeps Zone.js but throttles change-detection cycles to once per frame
It requires every component to use the default (non-OnPush) strategy
It disables change detection entirely, so views must be updated manually
questions.explanation: Zoneless CD (`provideZonelessChangeDetection()`) removes Zone.js and drives CD via signals/events directly — smaller bundle, fewer spurious cycles — stable since v20.2 and the default from v21. It does not keep Zone.js or disable CD.
